coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of carbon dots into polyvinyl alcohol/corn starch based film and its application on shiitake mushroom preservation

coredata.dc:description: Developing eco-friendly edible packaging films with multi-functional properties is highly required. This study involved synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) from dragon fruit, then incorporating them into a composite film based on polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)/corn starch (CS) to create a functional package to extend the shelf life of fresh shiitake mushrooms. Functional composite films with varying levels of CDs were formulated. The films' characteristics of morphology, mechanical properties, antioxidant properties, etc. were then determined, as well as their preservation effect on the fresh shiitake mushrooms. The results showed that the PVA/CS/CDs composite film showed excellent mechanical property, Ultraviolet (UV) barrier capability, antioxidant and antimicrobial properties. Specifically, addition of 8 mg/mL CDs in the composite films reduced weight loss of shiitake mushrooms by 30.74 %, decreased the decline in soluble solids content by 10.48 %, and halved the reduction in total sugar content after a 7-day storage period compared to films without added CDs. Furthermore, the films effectively lowered the respiratory intensity and browning of the mushrooms. This research demonstrates that CDs can serve as an effective component for the development of eco-friendly edible packaging films, as well as for their application in food preservation.

head.ce:title: Incorporation of carbon dots into polyvinyl alcohol/corn starch based film and its application on shiitake mushroom preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing eco-friendly edible packaging films with multi-functional properties is highly required. This study involved synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) from dragon fruit, then incorporating them into a composite film based on polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)/corn starch (CS) to create a functional package to extend the shelf life of fresh shiitake mushrooms. Functional composite films with varying levels of CDs were formulated. The films' characteristics of morphology, mechanical properties, antioxidant properties, etc. were then determined, as well as their preservation effect on the fresh shiitake mushrooms. The results showed that the PVA/CS/CDs composite film showed excellent mechanical property, Ultraviolet (UV) barrier capability, antioxidant and antimicrobial properties. Specifically, addition of 8 mg/mL CDs in the composite films reduced weight loss of shiitake mushrooms by 30.74 %, decreased the decline in soluble solids content by 10.48 %, and halved the reduction in total sugar content after a 7-day storage period compared to films without added CDs. Furthermore, the films effectively lowered the respiratory intensity and browning of the mushrooms. This research demonstrates that CDs can serve as an effective component for the development of eco-friendly edible packaging films, as well as for their application in food preservation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Shiitake mushrooms are the second largest edible mushroom in the world, accounting for about 25 % of the world's total edible mushroom production [1]. The storage period of fresh shiitake mushrooms is short because of their high water content, delicate tissues, strong respiration and transpiration, and the absence of protective structures on the surface, which makes them susceptible to mechanical damage and pathogenic bacterial infestation. Generally, after being picked and stored at room temperature for 2–3 days, wilting, open caps, rotting, and deterioration occur [2].

Carbon dots (CDs), nano-particle with diameters below 10 nm [3], are characterized by their exceptional solubility, photoluminescence, biocompatibility, stable aqueous dispersion, and high fluorescence intensity. These properties render CDs suitable for applications in optoelectronics, bioimaging, and environmental monitoring. More importantly, they also possess antioxidant properties, antimicrobial activity, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness [4–7].

The integration of CDs into the food packaging industry capitalizes on their unique properties, CDs can be utilized in coating solutions or composite films to enhance the preservation of perishable food items, such as fruits, vegetables, and meats, by providing protective barriers and potentially active monitoring of food quality and safety [8,9]. Chen et al. [8] reported that composite films prepared from CDs and chitosan inhibited Escherichia coli (E. coli) by up to 99.90 %; Guo et al. [9] fabricated a CDs incorporating film exhibiting excellent antibacterial properties. These results indicate that the CDs composite film exhibit excellent antimicrobial and antioxidant properties in the field of food preservation, and can substantially prolong the preservation period of a diverse range of foodstuffs.

Conventional shiitake mushroom preservation methods such as cooling and modified atmosphere packaging (MAP) have shown limitations, with cooling methods being energy-intensive and MAP often leading to off-flavors due to anaerobic respiration. The use of chemical preservatives, while effective, is not preferred due to potential health risks and environmental concerns [10]. The prevalent use of plastic film packaging to extend the shelf life of shiitake mushrooms has been accompanied by environmental challenges. The global proliferation of plastic films has contributed to pollution, endangering ecosystems due to their non-biodegradable composition [11]. Therefore, edible films, biodegradable biofilms and nanocomposite films have emerged as alternatives for shiitake mushrooms preservation. For instance, low surface energy polydimethylsiloxane /starch/poly adipate-butylene terephthalate films prolonged the shelf-life of packaged mushrooms by virtue of their excellent and appropriate barrier properties [12]; furthermore, cinnamaldehyde-tannic acid nanoemulsions /chitosan films showed excellent antimicrobial ability in preserving shiitake mushrooms [13], as well as tea polyphenol/chitosan/zearalanolysis protein films for their antioxidant effect [14]. However, to our best, the effect of CD-enhanced edible film on the preservation of shiitake mushrooms has not been reported.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and corn starch (CS) were chosen as the film-forming matrix for their complementary properties and eco-friendly. PVA offers excellent film-forming ability, water solubility, and biodegradability, while CS provides cost-effectiveness and forms strong films. Their combination yields a balanced structure with mechanical strength and barrier properties essential for food preservation. CDs synthesized from dragon fruit, a natural and sustainable source, enhance the film's eco-friendly and functionality due to their antioxidant and antimicrobial properties [15]. Using biodegradable materials like PVA, CS, and naturally derived CDs addresses the challenge of non-biodegradable plastic films by potentially reducing pollution and mitigating ecosystem damage [16].

In this study, CDs were synthesized from dragon fruit, and CD-enhanced PVA/CS composite films were used as a package. The study comprehensively determined the morphology, mechanical properties, water resistance, thermal properties, UV-blocking properties, antioxidant properties, and antimicrobial properties of the composite films. The impact of nanotechnology-based packaging on the biological characteristics and overall condition of fresh shiitake mushrooms was subsequently examined, and the process by which the films mitigate the senescence of these fungi was likewise scrutinized. Furthermore, it has been demonstrated to notably prolong the viability of numerous food items.

Fresh Lentinula edodes 808 and dragon fruits were selected from nearby supermarkets as whole, undamaged, of uniform maturity and size, and free from pests and diseases. CS was purchased from a nearby supermarket. PVA was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co. Anhydrous ethanol was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co.

CDs were prepared using the reported method with some modifications [6]. Thirty g of dragon fruit was mixed with 20 mL of 30 % alcohol, heated to 180 °C, and kept in a Teflon-equipped hydrothermal reactor for 6 h. After cooling, the reaction product was centrifuged at 3100 ×g for 20 min, and then the supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter. The filtrate was dialyzed using a 500 Da dialysis membrane and then freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder.

The structure and dimensions of the CDs were examined using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN analyzer. The carbon film-covered copper mesh was initially coated with a solution of CDs. Once the solvent had completely evaporated, the mesh was mounted onto a sample rod and then placed into the sample chamber of a TEM. Under an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, bright-field high-definition images of the CDs were subsequently captured. Ultimately, the shape and dimensions of the CDs were assessed employing image analysis software.

The surface groups of CDs were investigated using an FT-IR spectrometer (VECTOR22, Bruker USA, Inc). The CDs samples were thoroughly mixed with KBr powder at a 1:100 ratio in an agate mortar to form a homogeneous mixture, which was then pressed into a transparent test pellet. The pellet was then placed into the sample chamber of the FT-IR spectrometer. The FT-IR spectroscopy was utilized to collect the spectra of the CDs. The scanning parameters were set to cover a range from 400 to 4000 cm−1, with a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1 and a scanning speed of 32 scans per second.

The crystallographic properties of the CDs were characterized using an X-ray diffractometer (SmartLab9KW, Rigaku, Japan). The sample was uniformly spread on an XRD specific sample holder and tested under conditions with a scanning range of 10°–80° and a scanning speed of 5° per minute.

Aqueous solutions of CDs were analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fluoromax-4, Horiba, France). To ensure clarity and bubble-free conditions, the CDs aqueous dispersion was introduced into a quartz cuvette within the fluorescence spectrophotometer, and then it was transferred to the fluorescence spectrophotometer, with a photomultiplier voltage of 400 V and slit widths of 10 nm for both excitation and emission light, and the fluorescence spectrophotometer was employed to record the fluorescence spectrum of the sensor. The excitation wavelength was set at 375 nm, and the spectral range was from 300 to 480 nm.

The UV spectra of CDs were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Lambda25, PerkinElmer, USA). An aqueous solution of CDs was poured into a clean quartz cuvette to avoid air bubbles, the scanning wavelength range was set to 200–700 nm, moved to the sample chamber, and the data were saved after the scanning was completed.

The surface atomic states and compositions of CDs were detected using XPS (Axis Supra, Shimadzu, UK). The CDs samples were positioned on a sample stage designed for XPS, ensuring the sample surface was uniformly flat. Subsequently, the stage was inserted into the XPS instrument's sample chamber. Copper Kα radiation was selected as the excitation source, and the scanning angle range was set to 5–40°. Upon completion of the high-resolution XPS scan, the photoelectron spectroscopy data were collected and stored.

The scavenging capacity of CDs for DPPH radicals was determined as previously reported with some modifications [17]. 1 mL of CDs at varying concentrations (ranging from 64 to 8192 μg/mL) was combined with 1 mL of a 0.1 mM DPPH ethanol solution for the experiment. After 20 min of reaction in the dark, the absorbance at 517 nm was measured. The DPPH radical scavenging activity was obtained as follows:
(1)

where, Ai represents the absorbance of DPPH ethanol solution after reaction with CDs and A0 represents the absorbance of DPPH ethanol solution.

The ability of CDs to scavenge ·OH was determined by the Fenton reaction [18]. Mix 600 μL (1.8 mM) of ethanol salicylic acid solution and 200 μL (7.5 mM) of iron tetraoxide solution thoroughly, followed by the addition of 100 μL of CDs at different concentrations (64–8192 μg/mL). Then add 200 μL (200 mM) of hydrogen peroxide solution and 900 μL of deionized water. The reaction was conducted at 37 °C for 15 min, and the absorbance at 510 nm was measured. The ·OH radical scavenging activity was obtained as follows:

where, Aj represents the absorbance of the mixed solution after reaction with the CDs and A0 represents the absorbance of the mixed solution without the addition of the CDs.

The ability to scavenge
O 2 −
was performed according to the method of Zhao [19] with some modifications. Mix 500 μL (0.4 mM) of xanthine with 500 μL (0.24 mM) of nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT), and add 1 mL (0.049 unit/mL) of xanthine oxidase, mixing well. Then, add 500 μL of CDs solution at different concentrations (16–2048 μg/mL), and perform the reaction at 37 °C for 30 min. The absorbance was measured at 510 nm, and the superoxide anion radical scavenging activity was obtained as follows:

where, Ak represents the absorbance of the mixed solution after the reaction with the CDs and A0 represents the absorbance of the mixed solution without the addition of the CDs.

The antimicrobial properties of CDs were evaluated according to Chang et al. [20] with some modifications. S. aureus and E. coli were cultured in a liquid medium at 37 °C for an extended period to ensure activation. The optical absorbance of the microbial suspensions was normalized to a value of 0.1 at a wavelength of 600 nm, succeeded by a dilution factor of 100. The bacterial suspensions were incubated for 12 h after adding different concentrations of CDs solutions. Then, 100 μL of the bacterial suspension was taken and inoculated onto corresponding solid agar plates. After a 24 h incubation period at a temperature of 37 °C, photographs were taken of the bacterial colonies.

The films were prepared and modified according to the method reported by Lin et al. [21]. First, 2 g each of CS and PVA were combined in 250 mL of distilled water. This mixture was subjected to magnetic stirring for 25 min within a water bath maintained at 75 °C, ensuring complete dissolution of the CS. The temperature was then cooled down to 50 °C. Add 1.5 g of carboxymethylcellulose and continue to stir until it is completely dissolved. Afterwards, 2 mL of glycerol was added and stirred for 30 min to allow the ingredients to cross-link and form a homogeneous mixture. After the solution cools to room temperature, CDs solutions with concentrations of 4 mg/mL, 8 mg/mL, and 12 mg/mL, each containing 2 % (v/v), were added to the homogeneous mixture, followed by magnetic stirring for 20 min and ultrasonic treatment for 20 min at a power of 80 W. The prepared mixed solution was slowly and evenly poured onto a clean and smooth plastic plate, dried in an oven at 35 °C for 20 h, and then the composite film was subsequently placed on a light-avoiding plastic plate at 25 °C.

The composite films were ground into powder in a liquid nitrogen environment and subsequently analyzed by FT-IR according to the method described in Section 2.3. The composite films were tested by XRD according to the method in Section 2.3 after being glued to the XRD specific sample tray with rubber cement.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was utilized to scrutinize the nanoscale architecture of the composite films. Prior to analysis, the films were sectioned into smaller fragments, affixed to conductive adhesive, and coated with a layer of gold via sputtering under vacuum. Subsequently, the specimens were imaged using an electron microscope to capture micrographs that revealed the films' microstructure.

The WVP assessment was conducted in accordance with the protocol delineated by Feng [22]. Weight 5 g of anhydrous calcium chloride into a desiccated cylindrical weighing vial, measuring 50 mm in diameter and 30 mm in height. Place the film sample over the mouth of the bottle, weigh it, and put it into a constant temperature and humidity box at 25 °C with a relative humidity of 75 %. Weigh the sample after placing it in the box for 5 days. Calculate the WVP of the film samples as follows:
(4)

where, Δm is the mass of water transmitted through the sample (g), t is the placement time (h), A is the effective area of the sample through which the water is transmitted (m2), D is the film's depth (mm), and ΔP is the difference in water vapor pressure between the two sides of the film (Pa).

The composite films' thickness was determined using a micrometer for precise measurement. Eight points were randomly measured on the surface of the films, and the average value was taken in mm. The samples were cut into rectangular shapes of 60 mm × 20 mm, with an initial clamping distance of 40 mm and a tensile speed of 36 mm/min [23]. The tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) were determined by applying the subsequent formulas:
(5)

where, Fm is the maximum force (N), w is the width (mm) of the film strip and t is the film thickness (mm).

where, ΔL is the difference between the final length and the initial length (mm), and L0 is the initial length (mm) of film sample.

A film specimen measuring 20 mm × 30 mm was secured to a slide and positioned on a contact angle meter (OCA20, Germany). A micro syringe was utilized to deposit 10 μL of distilled water onto the film's surface. The instant the water droplets contacted the film was captured in a photograph, and the WCA was measured instantaneously.

The UV-blocking properties of the composite films were tested using the method provided by Bao et al. [8]. The composite films were cut to cuvette size and placed into the optical path with a scanning range of 200–600 nm to obtain transmission spectra. Finally, the spectral measurements were documented and subjected to analysis to assess the UV protective capabilities of the composite films.
Mix 4 mL of prepared ethanolic DPPH solution (0.1 mM) with 0.1 g of films at varying CDs concentrations and react in the dark for 30 min, following the method in Section 2.3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the PVA/CS/CDs composite films in shiitake mushrooms
ce:section.ce:para: Uniformly sized and mechanically intact shiitake mushrooms were selected and randomly divided into three groups, and all three groups were placed in paper cups with an aperture of 70 mm. The first group of shiitake mushrooms served as the control group, the second group of shiitake mushrooms was covered with a PVA/CS film, and the third group of shiitake mushrooms was covered with a composite film of PVA/CS/CDs, and all the shiitake mushroom samples were preserved for 7 days at 25 ± 2 °C. The quality of the samples was monitored daily during the preservation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test of qualities of shiitake mushrooms

ce:section.ce:para:
The method described by Mei et al. [6] was used to document the weight reduction of shiitake mushrooms, which was calculated as follows:
(8)
Weight loss percentage
%
=
M 1
−
M 0
M 0
× 100
where, M1 is the weight of shiitake mushrooms on the day of sampling, and M0 is the weight of shiitake mushrooms on the 0 th day.

The pH of the shiitake mushrooms was determined using an acidimeter (Testo 206pH2; Shanghai Baoshin Instrumentation Co., Ltd). The procedure was as follows: homogenize the mushroom sample, take an appropriate amount of the homogenized supernatant, apply it onto the calibrated electrode of the acidimeter, and wait for the readings to stabilize before recording the pH value.

Juice was first pressed from the mushroom sample. An appropriate amount of juice was then applied with a dropper to the measuring window of a hand-held refractometer (LH-Q20; Zhejiang Lu Xiang Environmental Science and Technology Co., Ltd.). Ensure the juice fully covers the measuring window, then turn on the refractometer and wait for a few seconds to read the value of soluble solids content on the scale.

The mushroom sample was homogenized, and the supernatant was obtained by filtration. An appropriate amount of supernatant was then sucked up with a burette and applied to the measuring window of a saccharimeter (WZ; Beijing Sunshine Yishida Technology Co., Ltd.), making sure there were no air bubbles and that the surface of the liquid was in full contact with the measuring window. After the reading on the meter stabilizes, record the value.
Daily increases in CO2 concentration were recorded using a 3051P respirometer, and respiratory intensity was expressed as mg CO2/Kg·h.

Vitamin C content in shiitake mushrooms was determined using 2,6-dichlorophenol indophenol titration [24]. The mushroom sample was first homogenized and the supernatant was obtained by centrifugation. Titration was carried out with a standard solution of 2,6-dichlorophenol indophenol and the volume of titrant consumed was recorded.

The hardness of the mushrooms was determined using a TA.XT Plus C texture analyzer (STRATE Micro Systems Ltd., UK). After placing the mushroom on the test rig, the P/75 probe was selected, the strain rate was set to 40.02 %, and the initial testing velocity was configured at 3030 mm/min, whereas the subsequent testing velocity was established at 60 mm/min.

Shiitake samples were tested for browning according to the method of Liu et al. [25]. Shiitake mushrooms were taken and chopped, 1 g (accurate to 0.001 g) of the sample was added to distilled water at a 1:20 ratio, homogenized in an ice bath, centrifuged at 25 °C and 8000 r/min for 5 min, and the absorbance was measured at 410 nm with distilled water as control, and the browning degree was expressed as A410 × 20.

A variance analysis was conducted on the collected data, with SPSS software utilized to assess the statistical differences among various factors and levels. P < 0.05 signifies a statistically significant difference, P < 0.01 denotes an extremely significant disparity. The graphical representations were created using Origin2021 software. Each experimental trial was executed in triplicate to ensure reliability.

The morphology and particle size of the CDs were observed using TEM. The CDs synthesized by the hydrothermal method showed uniform spherical shape and well dispersed (Fig. 1a). Fig. 1b demonstrated the uniform particle size distribution of these CDs, with all particles having diameter <6 nm, among which 2–3 nm particles accounted for 15.79 %, 3–4 nm particles accounted for 50 %, and 4–6 nm particles accounted for 34.21 %, respectively. In addition, the crystallite spacing of the CDs was 0.20 nm. These results are consistent with the previous findings of Pacquiao et al. [26].

FT-IR testing of CDs is mainly used to reveal their chemical structure and surface functional groups. The FT-IR spectrum, as depicted in Fig. 1c, exhibited characteristic stretching vibrations attributed to amine and hydroxyl groups within the spectral range of 2800 to 3800 cm−1. The subdued signal peak in this region suggested that the carbonization process during the synthesis of CDs was efficient [9]. Additionally, the vibrations observed near 1600 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching of CO and CC bonds, respectively. Furthermore, the vibrational bands near 1500 cm−1 are indicative of the stretching modes of CN bonds, while the peak observed near 1000 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of CO bonds [9]. The findings indicate that the CDs possess a plethora of functional groups on their surface. Such functional groups endow the CDs with enhanced solubility and facilitate the establishment of hydrogen bonding during film fabrication [9].

Fig. 1d illustrates the XRD pattern of the CDs, highlighting a peak at 25.9°, which is attributed to the (002) crystallographic planes characteristic of graphite [7]. Similarly, a broad diffraction peak at the carbon point at 2θ = 20.8° was found for CDs synthesized using Codonopsis pilosula [27].

The optical properties of CDs were investigated by UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1e, the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs exhibited a red-shift with varying excitation wavelengths, ranging from 300 nm to 480 nm, which probably was due to quantum effects and different emission trap sites on the CDs surface [28]. In addition, a distinct absorption peak was observed at 278 nm (Fig. 1f), which could be attributed to n-p and p-p* transitions in the CO and CC molecular bonds of CDs [29].

The full-scan XPS spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 2a) revealed three principal peaks positioned at 285, 400, and 532 eV, which are respectively ascribed to the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s orbitals. Furthermore, the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) predominantly featured peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.5 eV, corresponding to CC, CN, and CO bonds, respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) displayed two distinct peaks at 400.0 eV and 400.9 eV, corresponding to the nitrogen species associated with CNC, N(C)3, and NH, respectively. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) exhibited two characteristic peaks at 531.8 eV and 533.0 eV, which are attributed to the CO and COH/COC oxygen functionalities, respectively. In a similar vein, the XPS analysis of CDs synthesized from cocoon silk by Mei et al. [6] identified three primary peaks at 284, 399, and 532 eV, aligning with the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s orbitals, respectively.

CDs show great potential for application in the antioxidant field due to their unique optical properties and biocompatibility. CDs exhibited a significant dose-dependent scavenging of DPPH radicals, OH and
O 2 −
. Fig. 3a showed that the DPPH radical scavenging activity had markedly improved, increasing from 22.83 % to 86.45 % as the concentration of CDs was elevated from 64 μg/mL to 8192 μg/mL. Concurrently, the solution's color transitioned from dark purple to yellow. Similarly, the scavenging effect of OH was enhanced with increasing CDs concentration, with a decrease in absorbance and a change in solution color (Fig. 3b). Liu et al. [28] indicated that the primary cause of this phenomenon was the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs, OH being particularly significant. These hydroxyl groups served as hydrogen-bond donors, engaging in reactions with free radicals to produce stable complexes [28], leading to discoloration of the solution (Fig. 3d–e).

O 2 −
affects the freshness, quality and storage life of fruits and vegetables, but the antioxidant properties of CDs can effectively mitigate its negative effects [30]. Fig. 3c showed that when the concentration of CDs increased from 16 μg/mL to 2048 μg/mL, the color of the solution changed from pink to yellow. The proposed mechanism for CDs scavenging superoxide anion radicals involves an electron transfer process, which stabilizes the resultant molecules (Fig. 3f) [31].

S. aureus, a Gram-positive bacterium, and E. coli, a Gram-negative bacterium, are key pathogens responsible for causing foodborne illnesses and spoilage in fruits and vegetables. Their presence in fresh products can significantly compromise public health by leading to infections and diminishing the safety of the products [32]. Therefore, bacteriostatic substances need to be incorporated into cling film to extend shelf life and ensure food safety. The bacteriostatic effects of CDs were evaluated at concentrations of 0, 4, 8, and 12 mg/mL (Fig. 4a). The results demonstrated that the incorporation of CDs significantly suppressed bacterial colony growth compared to the control group, with the inhibitory effect was enhanced as the concentration of CDs increased. Compared with the control group, there were a small number of colonies on the surface of the medium with 4 mg/mL CDs added. Several individual colonies was observed on the surface of the medium with 8 mg/mL CDs added, and the inhibition rate reached 90 %, while there was basically no bacterial growth in the medium with 12 mg/mL CDs added. This probably because that CDs can be adsorbed onto the bacterial cell wall by diffusion and electrostatic interaction, thereby altering the permeability of the bacterial cell wall/membrane structure [33]. Under the photoexcitation of natural light, CDs effectively generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) (OH or 1O2) by activating oxygen in air or water [34]. When the level of ROS is elevated, it causes some irreversible damage to bacterial cells, such as the disruption of membrane structures such as cellular and mitochondrial membranes, oxidative damage to proteins and lipids, etc., and the breakage of DNA strands (Fig. 4b). Therefore, ROS cause oxidative damage to key biomolecules in the cell, leading to changes in or inhibition of the expression of important genes, and ultimately leading to cell death [35]. At a concentration of 12 mg/mL, CDs almost completely inhibited bacterial growth, which was attributed to their ability to produce ROS under light exposure, leading to oxidative stress and bacterial cell death [35]. Similarly, Alzahrani et al. [33] reported CDs for S. aureus and E. coli inhibition circles with diameters of 14 mm and 18 mm, respectively.

In the field of food packaging, ensuring that films have adequate mechanical strength and integrity is essential for protecting food during storage and transportation [36]. In this study, by introducing CDs into the composite films, the TS was significantly improved and the protection of food products was enhanced (Fig. 5a). The findings indicated that with an increase in the concentration of CDs, the TS of the films correspondingly improved. Notably, at a concentration of 8 mg/mL CDs, the tensile strength was observed to be 1.97 times greater than that of the films that did not contain any added CDs. However, when the concentration of CDs is too high, the aggregation of CDs destroys the network structure of the films, leading to a decrease in TS. Khoshkalampour et al. [37] revealed that the interfacial interactions and hydrogen bonding between PVA/CS and CDs were enhanced at a suitable CDs concentration, which enhanced the hardness of the films. However, high concentrations of CDs led to aggregation phenomena, which disrupted the network structure of the film solution and consequently reduced the TS of the films.

As shown in Fig. 5b, the EB of composite films was significantly enhanced by the addition of CDs, with a maximum 5.49-fold increase observed at a CDs concentration of 12 mL. This result indicates a substantial improvement in the films' flexibility and ductility, corroborating the influential role of CDs in modulating the mechanical properties of composite films. These findings are in accordance with prior studies [38], where the incorporation of CDs significantly enhanced the mechanical properties of PVA/CS films, primarily through the following actions: the formation of hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs with those in PVA and CS, which improves TS; the promotion of dispersion through π-π stacking of the aromatic structures on the surface of CDs, enhancing elongation at break; and the CDs serving as physical cross-linking points, increasing entanglement and thus improving overall mechanical stability [31].

As shown in Fig. 5c–f, SEM revealed that the films' surfaces were primarily smooth, with the addition of CDs leading to the presence of minor particles. This finding suggests that the CDs, despite their small size, can influence the surface morphology of the films, possibly through aggregation. This may be due to the direct contact of the film surface with air. Upon drying, the film experiences more rapid moisture evaporation from its upper surface compared to the lower one, leading to alterations in the intermolecular forces among the constituent elements. And water loss often leads to aggregation of CDs. Similar phenomena have been reported by Yuan et al. [39]. In comparison, the surface of the films containing 8 mg/mL CDs was smoother, suggesting that at this concentration, the CDs were found to be more evenly distributed throughout the film solution, which resulted in the formation of a more stable and uniform network structure within the solution.

The chemical interactions between PVA/CS and CDs were evaluated by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 6a). In PVA/CS films, OH, CH, and amide I vibrations of CO groups were observed at 3309 cm−1, 2947 cm−1 and 1735 cm−1. The absorption bands at 1458 cm−1 and 1421 cm−1 are attributed to CN and NH stretching vibrations [40,41]. In addition, absorption bands of CO stretching vibrations were observed at 1030 cm−1. FT-IR spectra showed that the CDs doped films did not change the frequency range of the spectrum, probably suggesting that CDs was fully mixed with the PVA/CS matrix to form compatible films [40,41].

The structural properties of the films with different concentrations of CDs were investigated by XRD spectroscopy (Fig. 6b). For the PVA/CS film, the diffraction peak is located at 2θ = 22.7°, which indicates the crystalline characteristics inherent in the PVA/CS blend [42]. The PVA/CS/CDs exhibited peaks at analogous positions, with no emergence of novel peaks. This observation may be attributed to the fact that the diffraction peaks of the CDs closely resembled those of the PVA/CS matrix, and the concentration of the CDs was relatively low. Consequently, it can be inferred that the incorporation of CDs did not induce any alterations in the crystalline framework of the PVA/CS blend [42].

Low WVP and WCA can improve the moisture barrier and hydrophobicity of the films, thus effectively keeping the freshness of the food and prolonging the shelf life [43]. Fig. 6c showed the WVP of the films decreased gradually with the increased concentration of CDs. Compared to the films without added CDs, the WVP of films incorporating 4 mg/mL, 8 mg/mL, and 12 mg/mL CDs demonstrated respective reductions of 4.61 %, 20.60 %, and 28.5 %, indicative of enhanced film densification. This might because the fact that the PVA/CS molecular chains interacted with dispersed CDs particles through hydrogen bonding [44]. Meanwhile, Fig. 6d showed that the contact angles of the PVA/CS/CDs films increased by 8.78 %, 21.81 %, and 11.08 %. Concurrently, as the concentration of CDs escalated from 4 mg/mL to 12 mg/mL, the comparative analysis revealed a progressive enhancement in performance relative to the CDs-free films. Hosseini's study [45] suggested that such alterations could originate from the CDs' incorporation, which bolstered the interaction with the film matrix, consequently diminishing the quantity of active hydrophilic groups present on the film's surface.

Edible commodities are susceptible to damage from UVA, 320–400 nm and UVB, 280–320 nm rays, leading to photooxidative reactions and the degradation of their constituent nutrients [46]. UV radiation is known to induce the formation of oxidative free radicals in food, leading to the degradation of nutrients, the development of off-flavors, rancidity, and discoloration [47]. These changes result in a significant reduction in food quality, underscoring the necessity for effective UV protection measures. Fig. 6e shows that the PVA/CS films have poor UV-blocking ability for 200–400 nm wavelengths, whereas films with added CDs showed the maximum increase in UV-blocking performance in the range of 200–400 nm, and the capability was increased with CDs concentration. Similarily, Bao et al. [48] also reported that UV-blocking performance of CDs in packaging films was concentration-dependent.

Oxidation plays a pivotal role in food spoilage, leading to substantial annual waste as a result of oxidation-induced decay. Consequently, incorporating antioxidants into packaging films, edible coatings, and films has emerged as a favored strategy to curb the oxidative deterioration of food items [49]. As shown in Fig. 6f, the antioxidant capacity of PVA/CS films progressively enhanced as the content of CDs increased, exhibiting a concentration-dependent trend. The DPPH activity reached a maximum scavenging of 45.61 %, which is an increase over the PVA/CS group by 68.30 %. In a study conducted by Zhao et al. [31], the antioxidant capabilities of PVA films enriched with CDs were scrutinized. The researchers applied a coating of PVA films, which incorporated 0.5 % CDs, onto bananas and monitored the oxidative browning that typically occurs during storage. Upon a six-day storage period, it was observed that the uncoated bananas had become discolored to a black hue, diminishing their marketability. In contrast, the bananas that were wrapped in PVA films containing CDs exhibited minimal blackening, indicating the potential of CDs to mitigate oxidation in food preservation [31]. This indicates that CDs act as antioxidants and limit oxidative reactions during storage, possibly because the CDs enhance antioxidant capacity by engaging in redox reactions, stabilizing reactive oxygen species, thereby alleviating oxidative stress on the film and the food it protects. The functional groups on the CDs can also chelate metal ions, a process that slows down the oxidation rate and thus extends the shelf life of packaged foods [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of composite films on the quality of shiitake mushroom
ce:section.ce:para: Given the outstanding TS, WCA, and antioxidant characteristics of the PVA/CS/CDs composite films, we proceeded to explore their efficacy in preserving mushrooms. Results showed that the PVA/CS/CDs composite film at 8 mg/mL had a good WCA and free radical scavenging ability, which was selected for subsequent testing, taking into account the impact of an elevated concentration of CDs on the film's hue and safety profile.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The appearance, weight loss and hardness of shiitake mushroom during storage
ce:section.ce:para: The surface integrity of shiitake mushrooms is a crucial indicator for evaluating their quality, given their inherently brief shelf life. The alteration in the mushrooms' appearance is depicted in Fig. 7a, initially, the shiitake mushrooms exhibited a uniformly brown surface upon storage commencement. Nonetheless, with the advancement of storage duration, the mushrooms underwent maturation, their surfaces developed wrinkles, and they experienced considerable water loss. After seven days of storage, the shiitake mushrooms left uncovered exhibited the greatest weight loss and were deemed unfit for commercial sale. Among the treated samples, those coated with PVA/CS composite films demonstrated superior preservation compared to the untreated control group. Furthermore, the mushrooms enveloped in PVA/CS/CDs composite films showcased the optimal preservation, retaining their appearance and quality more effectively.

Moisture content is one of the important indicators of the freshness of edible mushrooms [51]. During storage, the postharvest quality loss of shiitake mushrooms mainly includes the loss of water caused by transpiration, the formation of CO2 by respiration and the loss of organic matter [51]. Figs. 7b and 8e illustrate that the control group mushrooms experienced notable weight reduction and a decrease in firmness over the storage period of the samples. The mushrooms preserved with PVA/CS/CDs demonstrated superior retention of hardness and weight when contrasted with those in the control group. After 4 days of storage, the weight retention and hardness retention were 67.21 % and 70.11 % for PVA/CS/CDs and 67 % and 54 % for PVA/CS, respectively, while the weight retention and hardness retention in the control group were 47.02 % and 46.14 %, respectively. Xu et al. [52] reported that when the weight loss of the mushrooms reaches >70.10 %, the mushrooms may enter the glassy state where the mobility of the substance is greatly reduced, making the mushrooms hard [52], consistent with the results that the hardness of the mushrooms in the control group was abnormal from day 5 onwards. At the end of storage, the weight loss rate of the PVA/CS/CDs treatment group was significantly lower than that of the other treatment groups. Zheng et al. [53] have posited that the composite film's capacity to mitigate the rate of physiological biochemistry and respiration within the mushroom tissues is a pivotal mechanism. This modulation of internal gas exchange potentially restricts the efflux of water vapor, thereby attenuating the desiccation typically associated with post-harvest storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The TSS, pH and total sugars content of shiitake mushroom during storage
ce:section.ce:para: The quantification of TSS (sugars, organic acids, amino acids) constitutes a critical parameter in the preservation of mushrooms, as it significantly influences the palatability, nutritional content, and overall freshness [2]. Fig. 8a demonstrates the variation of TSS content of shiitake mushrooms with storage time under different treatments. The TSS in shiitake mushroom samples progressively declined as storage duration increased. In contrast, the PVA/CS/CDs group showed a 10.10 % increase in TSS compared to the control group. Yang et al. [54] reported that this may be due to the delayed dissolution of soluble solids by the CDs in the composite film, which formed a dense film on the surface of the samples and inhibited the microbial growth and respiratory metabolic rate of shiitake mushrooms during storage, thereby slowing down the decline in TSS. Consistently, the pH levels (Fig. 7c) and the content of total sugars (Fig. 8b) for mushrooms encapsulated in the PVA/CS/CDs composite film were more elevated compared to the control and the PVA/CS film groups. This observation suggests that the composite film possesses enhanced characteristics for food preservation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The respiration intensity, vitamin C content and browning index of shiitake mushroom during storage
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh shiitake mushrooms consume their own nutrients during the respiration process to provide energy for their substrates. The higher the respiration intensity, the faster the nutrient consumption and tissue aging, and the worse the storage resistance [55]. Fig. 8c illustrates that the respiration intensity of the three shiitake mushroom sample groups exhibited an initial increase followed by a subsequent decline. Li et al. [56] pointed out that the shiitake mushrooms continuing to metabolize post-harvest, with a surge in cellular activity and respiration rates initially. This heightened respiration is a cellular response aimed at sustaining life processes and repairing any trivial damage sustained during harvest. However, with the prolongation of storage time, the nutrients inside the shiitake mushroom were gradually depleted, and the intensity of respiration decreased. On the 7th day, the freshness preservation effect of the control and PVA/CS groups was poor, and the respiration intensity of the PVA/CS/CDs treated group was 27.01 % higher than that of the control group. Priyadarshi et al. [57] found that CDs could enhance the performance of the freshness preservation film, reduce the transmission rate of oxygen and water vapor, and enhance the antimicrobial property, thus slowing down the metabolism of shiitake mushrooms.

Vitamin C, as an antioxidant, in shiitake mushrooms helps to protect cells from oxidative damage and extends the shelf life of shiitake mushrooms [58]. The vitamin C levels in all groups diminished over storage time, with the shiitake mushrooms in the control group consistently exhibiting lower vitamin C content compared to those treated with PVA/CS and PVA/CS/CDs films (Fig. 8d). After 7 days of storage, the vitamin C content in the control, PVA/CS, and PVA/CS/CDs groups decreased by 66.80 %, 54.59 %, and 48.10 %, respectively. The decline in vitamin C content became less pronounced with the increase in CDs concentration, which probably because that CDs could enhance the barrier effect of the composite film, reduce the oxygen content of mushrooms, and reduce the oxidation of vitamin C, thus reducing vitamin C depletion [59].

Browning not only affects the appearance of shiitake mushrooms and reduces their commercial value, but also may be harmful to human health after consumption [60]. Fig. 8f revealed that the browning index in the three mushroom groups exhibited an overall upward trend. The browning index in the control group was 1.3 times greater than in the PVA/CS group and twice as high as in the PVA/CS/CDs group, indicating that the incorporation of CDs exerted a significant inhibitory effect on mushroom browning index. Similarily, Yang et al. [54] revealed that CDs had abundant functional groups and good biocompatibility, and the compound film achieved good freshness preservation and reduced the contact with oxygen, which led to a slower increase in the degree of browning index.

In this study, we synthesized CDs with robust UV absorption, antioxidant, and antimicrobial capabilities, which were then integrated into a PVA/CS film to enhance shiitake mushroom preservation. This film not only effectively maintained the mushrooms' quality by preserving their moisture, firmness, and reducing weight loss and pH decline but also inhibited the respiratory rate and enzymatic browning. The incorporation of these multifunctional CDs presents an eco-friendly and efficient approach to food preservation, showing significant potential for application in the industry. However, the scalability and cost-effectiveness of the CDs synthesis process are still challenges that need to be addressed. Future work should focus on optimizing the synthesis to minimize costs and broaden the application range. Additionally, it is crucial to assess the environmental footprint of these composite films through life cycle assessments and to explore the development of intelligent packaging technologies with real-time monitoring features to further enhance their utility in food preservation.
